content.text: Euclidean distance (L2 distance) measures the straight-line distance between two points in vector space. It is intuitive from geometry and is the default distance metric in many algorithms (k-means, k-NN in sklearn), but for text embeddings it is usually the wrong choice — cosine similarity or dot product almost always outperforms it. Understanding why requires understanding what embedding magnitudes represent.
Definition
Unlike cosine similarity (range [−1, 1]), Euclidean distance has no fixed upper bound — it grows with the number of dimensions and with vector magnitude. Smaller distance = more similar.

import numpy as np

a = np.array([1.0, 0.0])
b = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
c = np.array([0.7, 0.7])

print(np.linalg.norm(a - b))  # 1.414 (90° apart)
print(np.linalg.norm(a - c))  # 0.548 (45° apart)

# L2 is also expressible via dot products — useful for FAISS
# ‖a - b‖² = ‖a‖² + ‖b‖² - 2(a·b)
print(np.sqrt(np.dot(a, a) + np.dot(b, b) - 2 * np.dot(a, b)))  # 1.414
The Magnitude Problem

Euclidean distance is sensitive to vector magnitude. Two vectors pointing in identical directions but at different distances from the origin will have a non-zero Euclidean distance, even though they represent the same semantic direction.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])    # ‖a‖ = 5
b = np.array([0.6, 0.8])    # ‖b‖ = 1 — same direction as a, different magnitude
c = np.array([-3.0, -4.0]) # ‖c‖ = 5 — opposite direction to a

print("L2(a, b):", np.linalg.norm(a - b))  # 4.0  — LARGE (different magnitude)
print("L2(a, c):", np.linalg.norm(a - c))  # 10.0 — even larger
print("cos(a, b):", cosine_similarity([a], [b])[0, 0])  # 1.0  — identical direction
print("cos(a, c):", cosine_similarity([a], [c])[0, 0])  # -1.0 — opposite

L2 says vectors a and b are far apart (4.0), but cosine says they're identical (1.0). For semantic similarity, cosine is correct: a and b encode the same concept. L2 would rank them as dissimilar, which is wrong.
When L2 and Cosine Give the Same Ranking
When all vectors are unit-normalised (‖v‖ = 1), L2 distance and cosine similarity give the same ordering:
Since 2 − 2cos(θ) is a monotone decreasing function of cos(θ), minimising L2 distance is equivalent to maximising cosine similarity when all vectors lie on the unit hypersphere. FAISS
IndexFlatL2
over normalised vectors produces the same ranking as
IndexFlatIP

import faiss, numpy as np

N, D = 5, 4
vecs = np.random.randn(N, D).astype(np.float32)
vecs_norm = (vecs / np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)).astype(np.float32)
query = np.random.randn(1, D).astype(np.float32)
query_norm = (query / np.linalg.norm(query)).astype(np.float32)

# L2 on normalised vectors
index_l2 = faiss.IndexFlatL2(D)
index_l2.add(vecs_norm)
_, ids_l2 = index_l2.search(query_norm, N)

# IP on normalised vectors (cosine equivalent)
index_ip = faiss.IndexFlatIP(D)
index_ip.add(vecs_norm)
_, ids_ip = index_ip.search(query_norm, N)

# Rankings are reversed (L2 ascending = IP descending)
print(ids_l2[0])         # e.g. [3, 1, 0, 4, 2]
print(ids_ip[0][::-1])   # e.g. [3, 1, 0, 4, 2] — same order
FAISS IndexFlatL2

FAISS's L2 index is useful when working with algorithms that expect Euclidean distances (such as k-means or certain graph-based algorithms), or when integrating with libraries that compute L2 by default.

import faiss, numpy as np

D = 128
vecs = np.random.randn(1000, D).astype(np.float32)
query = np.random.randn(1, D).astype(np.float32)

index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(D)
index.add(vecs)

# Returns SQUARED L2 distances (not L2 — avoids a sqrt per result)
distances, indices = index.search(query, k=5)
print(distances)    # e.g. [[12.4, 15.2, 17.1, 18.8, 19.3]]
print(indices)      # e.g. [[ 47, 832, 291, 66, 511]]

# Convert squared L2 to actual L2 if needed
l2_distances = np.sqrt(distances)
print(l2_distances)
FAISS L2 returns squared distances, not L2 distances. For k-NN ranking this makes no difference (monotone transformation), but if you use the raw value as a threshold, account for the squaring.
Choosing Between L2, Cosine, and Dot Product
Metric
Sensitive to magnitude
Good for text embeddings
Typical use case
L2 (Euclidean)
Yes
Only when normalised
k-means, graph-based algorithms
Cosine
No
Yes
Text retrieval, document similarity
Dot product
Yes (when not normalised)
Yes (when normalised)
Recommendation (two-tower), fast cosine

For most text embedding use cases, normalise vectors and use dot product (which equals cosine). L2 is worth considering only when a downstream algorithm (such as HDBSCAN or k-means) requires it — and even then, reduce to 50 dimensions with UMAP first to counteract the curse of dimensionality.

attrs.alt: Euclidean distance in 2D vector space and nearest-neighbour retrieval pipeline

content.text: What Euclidean Distance Is

Euclidean distance, also called L2 distance, measures the straight-line distance between two points in n-dimensional space — the same distance you would measure with a ruler in physical space. It is the most geometrically natural distance metric and the default distance in most vector databases and k-nearest-neighbour algorithms.
Formally, given two vectors
a
= (a₁, a₂, …, aₙ) and
b

= (b₁, b₂, …, bₙ) in ℝⁿ, the Euclidean distance is the square root of the sum of squared coordinate differences. The squared version (L2²) is often used in practice because it is cheaper to compute (avoids a square root) and preserves the same nearest-neighbour ranking.

In the context of generative AI and embedding models, Euclidean distance operates on high-dimensional dense vectors — typically 128 to 4096 dimensions — produced by encoder networks. The choice of distance metric is baked into how a model is trained: models trained with triplet loss using L2 produce embeddings where L2 reflects semantic proximity.
Why It Matters

Vector similarity search is the backbone of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), semantic search, recommendation systems, image retrieval, and anomaly detection. Every one of these pipelines must answer a single question: given a query vector, which stored vectors are most similar? The answer depends entirely on the chosen distance metric.

Euclidean distance is the native metric for many embedding models (e.g., OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 recommends cosine similarity, but CLIP image embeddings often use L2 in practice). Mismatching the metric to the model's training objective degrades retrieval quality — sometimes catastrophically. Understanding when L2 is correct versus when cosine similarity or dot product is better is a critical engineering decision.
FAISS IndexFlatL2 is one of the most widely deployed vector search data structures, making L2 the de facto default in many vector database implementations
L2 is the Euclidean distance and the native objective in k-means clustering, t-SNE, and UMAP dimensionality reduction
Many contrastive learning frameworks (SimCLR, BYOL) use L2 distance in their loss functions, shaping the embedding space around this metric
Benefits of Euclidean Distance
Geometric intuition:
Maps directly to physical distance; easy to reason about in low-dimensional visualizations.
Magnitude-aware:
Unlike cosine similarity, L2 captures both direction and magnitude differences — important when magnitude carries meaning.
Native FAISS support:
IndexFlatL2 and its approximate variants (IndexIVFFlat, IndexHNSWFlat) are among the most optimized ANN implementations available.
Works with squared distance:
Squaring removes the expensive sqrt() without changing the nearest-neighbour ranking, yielding ~5–15% speed improvement.
Differentiable:
L2² is smooth everywhere, making it a natural loss component in metric learning (triplet loss, contrastive loss).
Trade-offs and Limitations

The primary weakness of Euclidean distance is its sensitivity to vector magnitude. Two sentences with very different lengths will have different embedding magnitudes if the encoder uses magnitude to encode confidence or frequency. If you compare a short query embedding against long passage embeddings, the longer passages will tend to be systematically farther away even if semantically relevant.

Euclidean distance also suffers from the curse of dimensionality more severely than cosine similarity. As dimensionality grows, the ratio of the maximum to minimum pairwise distance approaches 1 — distances become increasingly uniform and lose discriminative power. This effect is measurable at dimensions above ~1000 in practice.
Issue
Impact
Mitigation
Magnitude sensitivity
High — systematic bias against long/short vectors
L2-normalise vectors → cosine equivalent
Curse of dimensionality
Medium — degrades at d > 1000
Use PCA/PQ before indexing; or switch to cosine
Not scale-invariant
Medium — different embedding scales across batches
Normalise to unit sphere if scale is irrelevant
Slower than dot product
Low — sqrt is expensive at scale
Use squared L2 when relative order suffices
Training-metric mismatch
High — using L2 on cosine-trained model is wrong
Check model card for intended distance metric
How Euclidean Distance Works: Deep Mechanics
General Formula and Properties
For vectors a, b ∈ ℝⁿ, the L2 norm of their difference gives the Euclidean distance:

Three key metric axioms hold: non-negativity (d ≥ 0), symmetry (d(a,b) = d(b,a)), and the triangle inequality (d(a,c) ≤ d(a,b) + d(b,c)). The triangle inequality is what makes index structures like ball trees and M-trees possible — they use it to prune entire subtrees during search.
Relationship to Dot Product and Cosine
Expanding the squared Euclidean distance reveals its connection to dot product and norms:
When both vectors are L2-normalised (‖a‖ = ‖b‖ = 1), this simplifies to:

This shows that on the unit hypersphere, L2 and cosine similarity give identical nearest-neighbour rankings. The equivalence means you can always use FAISS IndexFlatL2 even when you conceptually want cosine similarity — just normalise your vectors first.
Computational Cost

Computing L2 distance between a query and N database vectors requires O(N·d) operations — linear in both N and dimension d. This is identical for cosine and dot product. The difference lies in how approximate algorithms exploit the metric structure: L2 naturally admits ball-tree and k-d tree decomposition, while dot product favours HNSW-style proximity graphs.
When to Use Euclidean Distance — and When Not To
Use Euclidean Distance When
The embedding model was trained with L2 loss (e.g., triplet networks with L2 norm, SimCLR with L2 distance in contrastive loss)
Vector magnitude carries semantic information (e.g., frequency-weighted word vectors where frequent words have larger norms)
You are using FAISS and want native, highly optimised index support
Working in low-to-medium dimensionality (< 512) where L2 retains discrimination
The application is clustering (k-means) or dimensionality reduction (t-SNE, UMAP) — both use L2 internally
Avoid Euclidean Distance When
The model card specifies cosine similarity (most text embedding models: OpenAI, Sentence-BERT, Cohere)
Comparing vectors of very different magnitude — normalise first or use cosine
Dimensionality exceeds ~1000 and ANN recall is degrading
You need a score in [0, 1] range — cosine similarity is more naturally interpretable
How to Implement: Step-by-Step
Step 1 — Pure NumPy

import numpy as np

# Two embedding vectors (e.g., 384-dimensional sentence embeddings)
a = np.array([0.2, -0.5, 0.8])  # simplified 3D example
b = np.array([0.9,  0.1, 0.3])

# Method 1: explicit
diff = a - b
l2 = np.sqrt(np.sum(diff ** 2))
print(f"L2 distance: {l2:.4f}")

# Method 2: numpy built-in
l2_np = np.linalg.norm(a - b)
print(f"L2 (numpy):  {l2_np:.4f}")

# Method 3: squared L2 (avoids sqrt — same nearest-neighbour ranking)
l2_sq = np.sum((a - b) ** 2)
print(f"L2² (cheap): {l2_sq:.4f}")
Step 2 — Batch Distance Matrix

import numpy as np

def pairwise_l2(A: np.ndarray, B: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute pairwise L2 distances between rows of A (n x d) and B (m x d).
    Returns (n x m) distance matrix.
    Uses the identity ||a-b||^2 = ||a||^2 + ||b||^2 - 2*a@b^T for efficiency.
    """
    # (n,) and (m,) squared norms
    a_sq = np.sum(A ** 2, axis=1, keepdims=True)   # (n, 1)
    b_sq = np.sum(B ** 2, axis=1, keepdims=True).T  # (1, m)
    cross = A @ B.T                                  # (n, m)
    dist_sq = a_sq + b_sq - 2 * cross
    # clip to 0 for numerical stability (floating point can give tiny negatives)
    return np.sqrt(np.clip(dist_sq, 0, None))

# Example: 5 queries vs 1000 database vectors, 128 dimensions
queries = np.random.randn(5, 128).astype(np.float32)
db = np.random.randn(1000, 128).astype(np.float32)

dists = pairwise_l2(queries, db)    # (5, 1000)
top3_idx = np.argsort(dists, axis=1)[:, :3]
print("Top-3 nearest indices per query:")
print(top3_idx)
Step 3 — FAISS IndexFlatL2 for Large-Scale Search

import numpy as np
import faiss  # pip install faiss-cpu

d = 128           # embedding dimension
N = 100_000       # database size

# Build the index
db_vectors = np.random.randn(N, d).astype(np.float32)
index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
index.add(db_vectors)
print(f"Index trained: {index.is_trained}, total vectors: {index.ntotal}")

# Search: 10 query vectors, return 5 nearest neighbours each
queries = np.random.randn(10, d).astype(np.float32)
distances, indices = index.search(queries, k=5)

# distances[i] = sorted squared L2 distances for query i (FAISS returns d²)
# indices[i] = indices of nearest neighbours
print("Nearest neighbour indices (query 0):", indices[0])
print("Squared L2 distances (query 0):     ", distances[0])
Real-World Example: Image Retrieval with CLIP Embeddings

CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pretraining) produces image embeddings where semantically similar images cluster together. A common image search pipeline embeds all gallery images offline, stores them in a FAISS index, then at query time embeds the search image and retrieves the nearest neighbours by L2 distance.

import numpy as np
import faiss

# Simulate CLIP image embeddings (real usage: clip.encode_image(images))
EMBED_DIM = 512
N_GALLERY = 50_000

np.random.seed(42)
gallery_embeddings = np.random.randn(N_GALLERY, EMBED_DIM).astype(np.float32)
gallery_ids = [f"img_{i:05d}.jpg" for i in range(N_GALLERY)]

# --- Offline: build the index ---
# For CLIP, L2-normalise first → L2 rank = cosine rank on unit sphere
norms = np.linalg.norm(gallery_embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)
gallery_norm = gallery_embeddings / norms

index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(EMBED_DIM)
index.add(gallery_norm)

# --- Online: handle a search query ---
query_embedding = np.random.randn(1, EMBED_DIM).astype(np.float32)
query_norm = query_embedding / np.linalg.norm(query_embedding)

k = 10
distances, idxs = index.search(query_norm, k=k)

print("Top-10 similar images:")
for rank, (idx, dist) in enumerate(zip(idxs[0], distances[0]), 1):
    cosine_sim = 1 - dist / 2  # convert L2² on unit sphere to cosine
    print(f"  {rank:2d}. {gallery_ids[idx]}  cosine={cosine_sim:.4f}")

The key insight in production CLIP systems: normalise embeddings to unit length before indexing. L2 distance on normalised vectors is equivalent to cosine similarity (d_L2² = 2(1 − cos θ)), so you get cosine semantics with FAISS's highly optimised L2 engine.
Common Mistakes
Mistake
What Goes Wrong
Correct Approach
Using L2 on cosine-trained embeddings without normalising
Magnitude differences distort retrieval ranking
Normalise to unit sphere first, or use IndexFlatIP (dot product)
Comparing raw L2 distances across models
Different models produce different magnitude scales
Use relative rankings, not absolute distance thresholds
Not using squared L2 when only ranking matters
Unnecessary sqrt() overhead at scale
l2_sq = np.sum((a-b)**2) is 10–15% faster
Forgetting float32 cast before FAISS
FAISS rejects float64; silent error or crash
Always cast: arr.astype(np.float32)
Using IndexFlatL2 for millions of vectors
Exact search is O(N·d) — too slow at scale
Use IndexIVFFlat or IndexHNSW for approximate search

# WRONG: comparing un-normalised OpenAI embeddings with L2
import openai
import numpy as np

# openai embeddings ARE normalised (unit norm), but custom model outputs may not be
emb_a = np.array([2.0, 4.0])  # magnitude 4.47
emb_b = np.array([1.0, 2.0])  # same direction, magnitude 2.24

l2 = np.linalg.norm(emb_a - emb_b)   # 2.24 — says they differ!
cos = np.dot(emb_a, emb_b) / (np.linalg.norm(emb_a) * np.linalg.norm(emb_b))
print(f"L2: {l2:.3f}, cosine: {cos:.3f}")  # L2: 2.236, cosine: 1.000

# CORRECT: normalise first
emb_a_n = emb_a / np.linalg.norm(emb_a)
emb_b_n = emb_b / np.linalg.norm(emb_b)
l2_n = np.linalg.norm(emb_a_n - emb_b_n)
print(f"L2 normalised: {l2_n:.6f}")  # 0.000000 — correctly identical
Best Practices
Check your model's distance metric:
Read the model card. Most text embedding models (Sentence-BERT, OpenAI, Cohere) are trained for cosine; image models vary.
Normalise when in doubt:
L2 on normalised vectors = cosine similarity; you lose nothing and gain metric-safety.
Use squared L2 for ranking:
np.sum((a-b)**2) is strictly faster than np.linalg.norm(a-b) and preserves ranking order.
Float32 always for FAISS:
FAISS requires float32. Cast both query and database arrays before indexing.
Switch to approximate search beyond 100k vectors:
IndexIVFFlat (quantised) or IndexHNSW (graph) reduce query time from O(N) to O(log N) or O(√N).
Set nprobe for IVF indexes:
index.nprobe = 32 balances recall vs. speed; default (nprobe=1) gives poor recall.
Performance and Scalability

Exact L2 search (IndexFlatL2) scales linearly with database size — querying 1M 128-d vectors takes ~50ms on a modern CPU. At 10M vectors it becomes impractical for latency-sensitive applications. Approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) methods bring this to < 10ms with > 95% recall@10.
Index Type
Build Time
Query Time
Recall@10
Memory
IndexFlatL2 (exact)
Instant
O(N·d)
N×d×4 bytes
IndexIVFFlat (k-means)
Minutes
O(√N·d)
N×d×4 bytes
IndexHNSWFlat (graph)
Slow
O(log N·d)
N×(d+graph) bytes
IndexIVFPQ (compressed)
Minutes
N×(d/8) bytes

import faiss
import numpy as np

d, N = 128, 1_000_000
xb = np.random.randn(N, d).astype("float32")

# Approximate: IVF with 4096 clusters
nlist = 4096
quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantizer, d, nlist, faiss.METRIC_L2)
index.train(xb)
index.add(xb)

index.nprobe = 64     # search 64/4096 cells — tune for recall vs. latency
xq = np.random.randn(10, d).astype("float32")
D, I = index.search(xq, k=10)
print("Done. Indices shape:", I.shape)
Tooling and Ecosystem
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search):
The gold standard for CPU/GPU exact and approximate L2 search. Supports IndexFlatL2, IVF, HNSW, PQ. pip install faiss-cpu.
ScaNN (Google):
Anisotropic quantization-based ANN; often outperforms FAISS at high recall. Available as pip install scann.
Annoy (Spotify):
Tree-based ANN; read-optimised and memory-mappable. Good for static indexes loaded into RAM. pip install annoy.
Hnswlib:
Pure-HNSW implementation with Python bindings; excellent recall/speed trade-off. pip install hnswlib.
Chroma / Pinecone / Weaviate / Qdrant:
Vector databases with REST APIs; support L2 alongside cosine and dot product. Wrap FAISS or HNSW internally.
sklearn NearestNeighbors:
Exact L2 search with a scikit-learn API — fine up to ~100k vectors for prototyping.

# Hnswlib example — efficient HNSW with L2 metric
import hnswlib
import numpy as np

dim, max_elements = 128, 10_000
p = hnswlib.Index(space='l2', dim=dim)
p.init_index(max_elements=max_elements, ef_construction=200, M=16)

data = np.random.randn(max_elements, dim).astype(np.float32)
labels = np.arange(max_elements)
p.add_items(data, labels)

p.set_ef(50)  # query-time exploration factor (≥ k)
query = np.random.randn(5, dim).astype(np.float32)
labels_out, distances = p.knn_query(query, k=5)
print("Nearest neighbours:", labels_out)
print("L2 distances:      ", distances)  # HNSW returns squared L2
Security and Reliability

Vector indexes are production infrastructure and carry reliability risks distinct from model code. An IndexFlatL2 holding millions of embeddings can consume tens of gigabytes of RAM. Memory over-allocation crashes the retrieval service silently if the pod OOM-kills without a graceful error.

A subtler issue: approximate indexes (IVF, HNSW) can return stale results after index updates if the build-query interleaving is not managed correctly. Additions to an IVF index invalidate cluster membership, reducing recall until re-clustering. Production systems typically version indexes and swap atomically rather than mutating live indexes.
Validate embedding dimensions at ingest:
FAISS will silently accept wrongly-shaped arrays in some builds; assert db.shape[1] == index.d before adding.
Monitor recall with a golden query set:
After index rebuilds, verify recall@10 against brute-force results on a sample of known queries.
Use float32, not float64:
FAISS and hnswlib assume float32 — accidental float64 inputs cause undefined behaviour or crashes.
Snapshot indexes before updates:
For HNSW/IVF indexes, save the serialised index to object storage before adding new vectors.
Key Takeaways
Euclidean (L2) distance measures straight-line distance; it is magnitude-sensitive and preserves both direction and scale of embeddings.
On L2-normalised (unit-sphere) vectors, L2 distance and cosine similarity give identical nearest-neighbour rankings — ||a-b||² = 2(1 − cos θ).
Use L2 when your embedding model was trained with L2-based loss (triplet, contrastive); check the model card.
FAISS IndexFlatL2 is the most battle-tested L2 search engine; switch to IVF or HNSW beyond ~100k vectors for acceptable latency.
Always cast embeddings to float32 before FAISS operations; float64 inputs cause silent errors or crashes.
The curse of dimensionality weakens L2 discrimination above ~1000 dimensions; use PCA or product quantisation before indexing.
For production indexes: version snapshots, monitor recall on a golden query set, and validate embedding dimensions at ingest.
